stem: The matrix \( M = \dfrac{1}{[[h]]}\begin{bmatrix} [[p]] & [[nsq]] \\ [[q]] & [[sp]] \end{bmatrix} \) is orthogonal (its columns are orthonormal). What is \( \det(M) \)?
solution: Every orthogonal matrix has determinant \( \pm 1 \): \( +1 \) for a rotation, \( -1 \) for a reflection. Computing directly, \( \det(M) = \dfrac{([[p]])([[sp]]) - ([[nsq]])([[q]])}{[[h]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the determinant of the opposite type of orthogonal matrix (rotation vs. reflection).
An orthogonal matrix is invertible, so its determinant cannot be \( 0 \).
The determinant of an orthogonal matrix has absolute value \( 1 \), never \( 2 \).
This multiplies only the diagonal entries, forgetting to subtract the off-diagonal product.
This takes only the off-diagonal product, forgetting the diagonal product.
This mixes up the entries and fails to give \( \pm 1 \).

stem: The rotation matrix \( R = \dfrac{1}{[[h]]}\begin{bmatrix} [[p]] & -[[q]] \\ [[q]] & [[p]] \end{bmatrix} \) rotates the plane by an angle \( \theta \) with \( \cos\theta = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[h]]} \) and \( \sin\theta = \dfrac{[[q]]}{[[h]]} \). What is \( \mathrm{trace}(R) \)?
solution: For a rotation matrix, \( \mathrm{trace}(R) = 2\cos\theta \). Here \( \cos\theta = [[p]]/[[h]] \), so \( \mathrm{trace}(R) = 2([[p]])/[[h]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( 2\sin\theta \) instead of \( 2\cos\theta \).
This is only \( \cos\theta \) itself, without doubling it.
The trace of a rotation matrix is not always \( 0 \) (that would require \( \theta = 90^\circ \)).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is one more than the correct trace.
This is one less than the correct trace.

stem: The matrix \( M = \dfrac{1}{[[h]]}\begin{bmatrix} [[p]] & [[nsq]] \\ [[q]] & [[sp]] \end{bmatrix} \) is orthogonal (its columns are orthonormal), hence invertible. What is the rank of \( M \)?
solution: Every orthogonal matrix is invertible (its columns are orthonormal, hence linearly independent), so a \( 2\times 2 \) orthogonal matrix always has full rank \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: An invertible matrix cannot have rank \( 0 \).
Rank \( 1 \) would mean the columns are dependent, contradicting orthogonality.
The matrix is only \( 2\times 2 \), so its rank cannot exceed \( 2 \).
This is the normalizing denominator \( h \), not the rank.
This mixes up the Pythagorean-triple generators with the rank.
This mixes up the Pythagorean-triple generators with the rank.

stem: Rotation matrix \( R_1 = \dfrac{1}{[[h1]]}\begin{bmatrix} [[p1]] & -[[q1]] \\ [[q1]] & [[p1]] \end{bmatrix} \) rotates by angle \( \theta_1 \), and \( R_2 = \dfrac{1}{[[h2]]}\begin{bmatrix} [[p2]] & -[[q2]] \\ [[q2]] & [[p2]] \end{bmatrix} \) rotates by angle \( \theta_2 \). The product \( M = R_1 R_2 \) rotates by \( \theta_1 + \theta_2 \). What is \( \mathrm{trace}(M) \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses the cosine DIFFERENCE formula \( \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2+\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 \) (doubled) instead of the sum formula.
This is \( \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \) itself, without doubling it for the trace.
This uses \( 2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \) instead of \( 2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \).
The trace of a product of two rotations is not always \( 0 \).
This is one more than the correct trace.
This is one less than the correct trace.

stem: The \( 3 \times 3 \) block-diagonal matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[p]]/[[h]] & -[[q]]/[[h]] & 0 \\ [[q]]/[[h]] & [[p]]/[[h]] & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & [[e]] \end{bmatrix} \) is orthogonal (a \( 2\times 2 \) rotation block together with \( [[e]] \) on the third axis). What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: The \( 2\times 2 \) rotation block has determinant \( \left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[h]]}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{[[q]]}{[[h]]}\right)^2 = 1 \) (since \( [[p]]^2+[[q]]^2=[[h]]^2 \)), so \( \det(A) = 1 \cdot [[e]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the third-axis entry.
An orthogonal matrix is invertible, so its determinant cannot be \( 0 \).
The determinant of an orthogonal matrix has absolute value \( 1 \), never \( 2 \).
This reports a single matrix entry instead of the full determinant.
This is one more than the correct determinant.
This is two less than the correct determinant.
